attributes.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine health check. She has a medical history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her medications include metformin, perindopril, and amlodipine. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her blood pressure is 142/88 mmHg. Laboratory investigations reveal creatinine 120 μmol/L (45-90), estimated glomerular filtration rate 48 mL/min/1.73m² (>90), and albumin:creatinine ratio 45 mg/mmol (<3.5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following statements about chronic kidney disease is correct?
options: Lethargy is a rare symptom of chronic kidney disease
Serum creatinine is a sensitive early biomarker of kidney injury
Up to 90% of kidney function can be lost before symptoms develop
COVID-19 infection does not increase the risk of chronic kidney disease
Hypertension is a cause but not a consequence of chronic kidney disease
attributes.explanation:
Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is a significant health problem that often goes undetected due to its insidious nature. Up to 90% of kidney function can be lost before symptoms develop, which highlights the importance of screening at-risk populations. This asymptomatic nature of early CKD is one reason why regular monitoring of kidney function in patients with risk factors like diabetes and hypertension is crucial.

Lethargy is a rare symptom of chronic kidney disease is incorrect. In fact, fatigue and lethargy are common symptoms of CKD, particularly as the disease progresses, due to anaemia, metabolic derangements, and accumulation of uremic toxins.

Serum creatinine is a sensitive early biomarker of kidney injury is incorrect. Creatinine is actually a late marker of kidney dysfunction, only becoming elevated after significant kidney damage has occurred. A person can lose up to 50% of their kidney function before seeing a significant rise in serum creatinine.

COVID-19 infection does not increase the risk of chronic kidney disease is incorrect. There is growing evidence that COVID-19 infection can cause both acute kidney injury and increase the risk of developing CKD, particularly in cases of 'long COVID'.

Hypertension is a cause but not a consequence of chronic kidney disease is incorrect. Hypertension can be both a cause and a consequence of CKD, creating a vicious cycle. The kidneys play a crucial role in blood pressure regulation through the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.
learningPoints: Chronic kidney disease is often asymptomatic until late stages with up to 90% loss of function.
Serum creatinine is a late marker that only rises after significant kidney damage has occurred.
Hypertension can be both a cause and consequence of chronic kidney disease.
references: Jdiaa SS, Mansour R, El Alayli A, Gautam A, Thomas P, Mustafa RA. COVID-19 and chronic kidney disease: an updated overview of reviews. J Nephrol. 2022 Jan;35(1):69-85. doi: 10.1007/s40620-021-01206-8. Epub 2022 Jan 11. PMID: 35013985; PMCID: PMC8747880.

Jun M, Wick J, Neuen BL, Kotwal S, Badve SV, Woodward M, Chalmers J, Peiris D, Rodgers A, Nallaiah K, Jardine MJ, Perkovic V, Gallagher M, Ronksley PE. The Prevalence of CKD in Australian Primary Care: Analysis of a National General Practice Dataset. Kidney Int Rep. 2023 Nov 29;9(2):312-322. doi: 10.1016/j.ekir.2023.11.022. PMID: 38344725; PMCID: PMC10851013

Radford J, Kitsos A, Stankovich J, Castelino R, Khanam M, Jose M, Peterson G, Saunder T, Wimmer B, Razizaidi T. Epidemiology of chronic kidney disease in Australian general practice: National Prescribing Service MedicineWise MedicineInsight dataset. Nephrology (Carlton). 2019 Oct;24(10):1017-1025. doi: 10.1111/nep.13537. Epub 2018 Dec 13. PMID: 30467996; PMCID: PMC6790712
Hoy WE, Mott SA, McDonald SP. An update on chronic kidney disease in Aboriginal Australians. Clin Nephrol. 2020 Supplement-Jan;93(1):124-128. doi: 10.5414/CNP92S122. PMID: 31426912
